name: Acute Kidney Injury
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of kidney diseases.
overview: Acute Kidney Injury is a disease affecting the kidneys, the organs responsible for filtering waste products, excess water, and impurities from the blood. It impairs the kidney's glomerular or tubular structures, leading to fluid retention, electrolyte imbalances, and buildup of toxins. It affects the body by causing edema, hypertension, and fatigue.

causes_detail.primary: Damage to the nephrons (filtering units) due to systemic disease, autoimmune attack, or infection in Acute Kidney Injury.
causes_detail.secondary: Glomerular inflammation or physical obstruction in the urinary tract.
causes_detail.environmental: Chronic exposure to heavy metals, dehydration in hot climates, or high dietary sodium.
causes_detail.genetic: Inherited mutations such as in Polycystic Kidney Disease (PKD).

early: Mild swelling in ankles or around eyes
common: Pain in areas surrounding Acute Kidney Injury
severe: Severe shortness of breath (fluid in lungs)
Persistent vomiting and severe loss of appetite
Severe decreased output or lack of urine

risk_factors_detail.age: Risk increases in older adults (60+) due to natural decline in nephron function.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: High-sodium diet, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, and high intake of processed foods.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: Family members with chronic kidney failure, diabetes, or severe hypertension.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Type 1 or 2 Diabetes, chronic High Blood Pressure, history of kidney stones, or cardiovascular disease.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Frequent exposure to chemical solvents or chronic overuse of over-the-counter painkillers (NSAIDs).

complications.untreated: End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) requiring dialysis, fluid overload, severe hyperkalemia (high potassium, dangerous for the heart), or death.
complications.long_term: Chronic anemia, mineral bone disease (weak bones), fluid accumulation in the heart/lungs, and accelerated cardiovascular disease.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Keep blood sugar and blood pressure under control; do not smoke; maintain a healthy weight.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Treat urinary tract infections promptly to prevent bacteria from climbing to the kidneys.
prevention_detail.vaccination: Get vaccinated against Hepatitis B (dialysis patients are at risk) and receive annual flu shots.
prevention_detail.dietary: Follow a moderate-protein, low-sodium, low-phosphorus diet if kidney function is compromised.
prevention_detail.exercise: Engage in regular aerobic exercise to support blood pressure management.

precautions_detail.daily: Limit sodium intake to under 2,000 mg/day; avoid over-the-counter NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Naproxen); monitor daily fluid intake.
precautions_detail.travel: Locate travel-dialysis options ahead of time if on dialysis; carry medical records and potassium-restricting food lists.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in early kidney screening programs (urine albumin and blood creatinine checks).

treatment_detail.first_line: Blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors or ARBs) to protect kidney function, and dietary changes.
treatment_detail.home_care: Manage fluid intake, track daily weights, limit potassium and sodium-rich foods.
treatment_detail.medical: Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis), erythropoietin injections (for anemia), or kidney transplantation.

when_to_consult: Consult a doctor for persistent foamy urine, swelling in ankles or eyes, or recurrent burning during urination.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care for severe chest pain or shortness of breath, severe confusion, complete inability to urinate, or cardiac arrhythmias.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Checking blood pressure, examining ankles/legs for pitting edema, and listening to heart and lungs.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Serum Creatinine and Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR), Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN), and Urinalysis (checking for albumin/protein).
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Renal Ultrasound to inspect size and blockages; CT scan of kidneys.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Urine microalbuminuria test and eGFR blood tests for diabetic patients.

eat: Egg whites and skinless chicken breast
White rice and pasta (if phosphorus restriction is active)
avoid: Canned foods and high-sodium packaged snacks
Whole wheat bread and brown rice (high phosphorus/potassium)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Perform moderate walking or cycling. Avoid extreme, dehydrating workouts that can cause rhabdomyolysis.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Ensure 7-8 hours of sleep; sleep disturbances are common in advanced kidney disease.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in stress reduction as stress hormones raise blood pressure, damaging kidney blood vessels.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink adequate water (1.5-2L) to support filtration. Limit fluids strictly only if advised by a nephrologist due to advanced failure.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Acute kidney injury can resolve in weeks with treatment, whereas chronic kidney disease is permanent but progression can be slowed over years.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Get eGFR and urine protein checked regularly. Keep a tight log of daily blood pressure.

faqs.question: What is eGFR?
faqs.answer: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) measures how well your kidneys filter waste. A value below 60 for more than 3 months indicates chronic kidney disease.

faqs.question: Why does kidney disease cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Healthy kidneys produce a hormone called erythropoietin (EPO), which signals the bone marrow to make red blood cells. Damaged kidneys make less EPO, leading to anemia.

faqs.question: Are painkillers dangerous for the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like Ibuprofen, Naproxen, and high-dose Aspirin restrict blood flow to kidneys and can cause acute damage.

faqs.question: Can kidney stones lead to kidney disease?
faqs.answer: Occasional stones rarely cause permanent damage. However, recurrent stones causing long-term blockages and infections can impair kidney function.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis?
faqs.answer: Hemodialysis filters blood using an external machine, usually at a clinic. Peritoneal dialysis uses the lining of your abdomen to filter waste at home.

awareness_tips: Keep your blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg to protect renal capillaries.
Get checked for kidney disease if you have diabetes or hypertension.
Limit daily salt intake and choose herbs/spices for flavoring instead.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis?
faqs.answer: Hemodialysis filters blood using an external machine, usually at a clinic. Peritoneal dialysis uses the lining of your abdomen to filter waste at home.

awareness_tips: Keep your blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg to protect renal capillaries.
Get checked for kidney disease if you have diabetes or hypertension.
Limit daily salt intake and choose herbs/spices for flavoring instead.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

causes: Genetic predisposition factors associated with Glomerulonephritis
Environmental triggers and exposure related to Glomerulonephritis
Immune system irregularities
precautions: Stay hydrated and get adequate rest
Consult a physician if symptoms worsen
prevention: Maintain a balanced, healthy lifestyle
Routine screening and checkups with healthcare providers
Wash hands regularly and maintain hygiene
basic_treatment: Symptomatic support (adequate hydration, rest)
Over-the-counter pain relief as advised by doctors
Therapy or specialist referral depending on severity

name: Nephrotic Syndrome
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of kidney diseases.
overview: Nephrotic Syndrome is a disease affecting the kidneys, the organs responsible for filtering waste products, excess water, and impurities from the blood. It impairs the kidney's glomerular or tubular structures, leading to fluid retention, electrolyte imbalances, and buildup of toxins. It affects the body by causing edema, hypertension, and fatigue.

causes_detail.primary: Damage to the nephrons (filtering units) due to systemic disease, autoimmune attack, or infection in Nephrotic Syndrome.
causes_detail.secondary: Glomerular inflammation or physical obstruction in the urinary tract.
causes_detail.environmental: Chronic exposure to heavy metals, dehydration in hot climates, or high dietary sodium.
causes_detail.genetic: Inherited mutations such as in Polycystic Kidney Disease (PKD).

early: Mild swelling in ankles or around eyes
common: Pain in areas surrounding Nephrotic Syndrome
severe: Severe shortness of breath (fluid in lungs)
Persistent vomiting and severe loss of appetite
Severe decreased output or lack of urine

risk_factors_detail.age: Risk increases in older adults (60+) due to natural decline in nephron function.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: High-sodium diet, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, and high intake of processed foods.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: Family members with chronic kidney failure, diabetes, or severe hypertension.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Type 1 or 2 Diabetes, chronic High Blood Pressure, history of kidney stones, or cardiovascular disease.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Frequent exposure to chemical solvents or chronic overuse of over-the-counter painkillers (NSAIDs).

complications.untreated: End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) requiring dialysis, fluid overload, severe hyperkalemia (high potassium, dangerous for the heart), or death.
complications.long_term: Chronic anemia, mineral bone disease (weak bones), fluid accumulation in the heart/lungs, and accelerated cardiovascular disease.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Keep blood sugar and blood pressure under control; do not smoke; maintain a healthy weight.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Treat urinary tract infections promptly to prevent bacteria from climbing to the kidneys.
prevention_detail.vaccination: Get vaccinated against Hepatitis B (dialysis patients are at risk) and receive annual flu shots.
prevention_detail.dietary: Follow a moderate-protein, low-sodium, low-phosphorus diet if kidney function is compromised.
prevention_detail.exercise: Engage in regular aerobic exercise to support blood pressure management.

precautions_detail.daily: Limit sodium intake to under 2,000 mg/day; avoid over-the-counter NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Naproxen); monitor daily fluid intake.
precautions_detail.travel: Locate travel-dialysis options ahead of time if on dialysis; carry medical records and potassium-restricting food lists.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in early kidney screening programs (urine albumin and blood creatinine checks).

treatment_detail.first_line: Blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors or ARBs) to protect kidney function, and dietary changes.
treatment_detail.home_care: Manage fluid intake, track daily weights, limit potassium and sodium-rich foods.
treatment_detail.medical: Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis), erythropoietin injections (for anemia), or kidney transplantation.

when_to_consult: Consult a doctor for persistent foamy urine, swelling in ankles or eyes, or recurrent burning during urination.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care for severe chest pain or shortness of breath, severe confusion, complete inability to urinate, or cardiac arrhythmias.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Checking blood pressure, examining ankles/legs for pitting edema, and listening to heart and lungs.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Serum Creatinine and Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR), Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN), and Urinalysis (checking for albumin/protein).
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Renal Ultrasound to inspect size and blockages; CT scan of kidneys.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Urine microalbuminuria test and eGFR blood tests for diabetic patients.

eat: Egg whites and skinless chicken breast
White rice and pasta (if phosphorus restriction is active)
avoid: Canned foods and high-sodium packaged snacks
Whole wheat bread and brown rice (high phosphorus/potassium)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Perform moderate walking or cycling. Avoid extreme, dehydrating workouts that can cause rhabdomyolysis.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Ensure 7-8 hours of sleep; sleep disturbances are common in advanced kidney disease.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in stress reduction as stress hormones raise blood pressure, damaging kidney blood vessels.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink adequate water (1.5-2L) to support filtration. Limit fluids strictly only if advised by a nephrologist due to advanced failure.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Acute kidney injury can resolve in weeks with treatment, whereas chronic kidney disease is permanent but progression can be slowed over years.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Get eGFR and urine protein checked regularly. Keep a tight log of daily blood pressure.

faqs.question: What is eGFR?
faqs.answer: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) measures how well your kidneys filter waste. A value below 60 for more than 3 months indicates chronic kidney disease.

faqs.question: Why does kidney disease cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Healthy kidneys produce a hormone called erythropoietin (EPO), which signals the bone marrow to make red blood cells. Damaged kidneys make less EPO, leading to anemia.

faqs.question: Are painkillers dangerous for the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like Ibuprofen, Naproxen, and high-dose Aspirin restrict blood flow to kidneys and can cause acute damage.

faqs.question: Can kidney stones lead to kidney disease?
faqs.answer: Occasional stones rarely cause permanent damage. However, recurrent stones causing long-term blockages and infections can impair kidney function.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis?
faqs.answer: Hemodialysis filters blood using an external machine, usually at a clinic. Peritoneal dialysis uses the lining of your abdomen to filter waste at home.

awareness_tips: Keep your blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg to protect renal capillaries.
Get checked for kidney disease if you have diabetes or hypertension.
Limit daily salt intake and choose herbs/spices for flavoring instead.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Keep blood sugar and blood pressure under control; do not smoke; maintain a healthy weight.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Treat urinary tract infections promptly to prevent bacteria from climbing to the kidneys.
prevention_detail.vaccination: Get vaccinated against Hepatitis B (dialysis patients are at risk) and receive annual flu shots.
prevention_detail.dietary: Follow a moderate-protein, low-sodium, low-phosphorus diet if kidney function is compromised.
prevention_detail.exercise: Engage in regular aerobic exercise to support blood pressure management.

precautions_detail.daily: Limit sodium intake to under 2,000 mg/day; avoid over-the-counter NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Naproxen); monitor daily fluid intake.
precautions_detail.travel: Locate travel-dialysis options ahead of time if on dialysis; carry medical records and potassium-restricting food lists.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in early kidney screening programs (urine albumin and blood creatinine checks).

treatment_detail.first_line: Blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors or ARBs) to protect kidney function, and dietary changes.
treatment_detail.home_care: Manage fluid intake, track daily weights, limit potassium and sodium-rich foods.
treatment_detail.medical: Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis), erythropoietin injections (for anemia), or kidney transplantation.

when_to_consult: Consult a doctor for persistent foamy urine, swelling in ankles or eyes, or recurrent burning during urination.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care for severe chest pain or shortness of breath, severe confusion, complete inability to urinate, or cardiac arrhythmias.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Checking blood pressure, examining ankles/legs for pitting edema, and listening to heart and lungs.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Serum Creatinine and Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR), Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN), and Urinalysis (checking for albumin/protein).
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Renal Ultrasound to inspect size and blockages; CT scan of kidneys.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Urine microalbuminuria test and eGFR blood tests for diabetic patients.

eat: Egg whites and skinless chicken breast
White rice and pasta (if phosphorus restriction is active)
avoid: Canned foods and high-sodium packaged snacks
Whole wheat bread and brown rice (high phosphorus/potassium)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Perform moderate walking or cycling. Avoid extreme, dehydrating workouts that can cause rhabdomyolysis.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Ensure 7-8 hours of sleep; sleep disturbances are common in advanced kidney disease.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in stress reduction as stress hormones raise blood pressure, damaging kidney blood vessels.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink adequate water (1.5-2L) to support filtration. Limit fluids strictly only if advised by a nephrologist due to advanced failure.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Acute kidney injury can resolve in weeks with treatment, whereas chronic kidney disease is permanent but progression can be slowed over years.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Get eGFR and urine protein checked regularly. Keep a tight log of daily blood pressure.

faqs.question: What is eGFR?
faqs.answer: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) measures how well your kidneys filter waste. A value below 60 for more than 3 months indicates chronic kidney disease.

faqs.question: Why does kidney disease cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Healthy kidneys produce a hormone called erythropoietin (EPO), which signals the bone marrow to make red blood cells. Damaged kidneys make less EPO, leading to anemia.

faqs.question: Are painkillers dangerous for the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like Ibuprofen, Naproxen, and high-dose Aspirin restrict blood flow to kidneys and can cause acute damage.

faqs.question: Can kidney stones lead to kidney disease?
faqs.answer: Occasional stones rarely cause permanent damage. However, recurrent stones causing long-term blockages and infections can impair kidney function.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis?
faqs.answer: Hemodialysis filters blood using an external machine, usually at a clinic. Peritoneal dialysis uses the lining of your abdomen to filter waste at home.

awareness_tips: Keep your blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg to protect renal capillaries.
Get checked for kidney disease if you have diabetes or hypertension.
Limit daily salt intake and choose herbs/spices for flavoring instead.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

name: Eczema
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of skin diseases.
overview: Eczema is a condition affecting the skin, the body's largest organ. It can involve inflammation, infection, allergic reactions, or abnormal cellular growths. It affects the body by compromising the skin barrier, causing itching, pain, rashes, scaling, or cosmetic distress, and making the body more vulnerable to secondary infections.

causes_detail.primary: Epidermal barrier dysfunction, autoimmune cellular rapid growth, or pathogen infestation causing Eczema.
causes_detail.secondary: Allergic contact sensitization, fungal colonization, or genetic skin cell overproduction.
causes_detail.environmental: Exposure to harsh chemicals, allergens, UV radiation, dry climates, or friction.
causes_detail.genetic: Hereditary factors influence conditions like Eczema, Psoriasis, and skin cancer susceptibility.

severe: Signs of skin infection (pus, warmth, red streaks)
Fever or chills accompanying skin peeling

risk_factors_detail.age: Acne is common in adolescents; eczema is frequent in children; skin cancers and shingles are more common in older adults.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: Frequent outdoor sun exposure without sunscreen, smoking, use of harsh soaps, and high stress levels.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: Family history of eczema, asthma, allergies, psoriasis, or melanoma.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: History of autoimmune disorders, chronic allergies, weakened immune system, or poor circulation.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Working with industrial chemicals, living in extremely dry or hot/humid climates, or exposure to allergens like pollen or pet dander.

complications.untreated: Secondary bacterial infections (cellulitis, impetigo), severe scarring, chronic pain, or systemic infection.
complications.long_term: Chronic skin thickening (lichenification), permanent pigment changes, sleep loss due to itching, or metastatic cancer.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen daily; avoid tanning beds; wear protective clothing outdoors.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Bathe daily with gentle, soap-free cleansers; moisturize immediately after bathing; do not share towels.
prevention_detail.vaccination: The Shingles (Zoster) vaccine prevents shingles outbreaks and chronic post-herpetic neuralgia.
prevention_detail.dietary: Stay hydrated and consume foods rich in healthy fats (Omega-3s) to support the lipid skin barrier.
prevention_detail.exercise: Shower promptly after sweating to prevent fungal or bacterial overgrowth on the skin.

precautions_detail.daily: Moisturize skin twice daily; perform self-skin checks; avoid scratching active lesions.
precautions_detail.travel: Pack hypoallergenic toiletries; bring adequate sun protection; carry hydrocortisone or prescribed topical creams.
precautions_detail.community: Promote sun safety in schools and participate in free skin cancer screening camps.

treatment_detail.first_line: Topical moisturizers, barrier creams, and mild topical hydrocortisone.
treatment_detail.home_care: Take lukewarm baths, use cool compresses for itching, and keep fingernails short to prevent scratching.
treatment_detail.medical: Prescription topical steroids, oral antihistamines, phototherapy, or systemic biologic therapies for severe autoimmune conditions.

when_to_consult: Consult a dermatologist for a rash that does not respond to OTC creams in 2 weeks, skin lesions that bleed, or changing moles.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care if a rash covers your entire body rapidly, is accompanied by high fever, begins blistering, or causes difficulty breathing.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Visual inspection of lesions, checking patterns and locations, and evaluating nail and scalp health.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Skin scraping for fungal culture, patch testing for contact allergies, or bacterial wound swab.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Dermoscopy (magnified skin inspection); rarely, ultrasound to check for deep abscesses.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Skin biopsy (taking a small tissue sample for lab analysis) and annual full-body mole mapping.

eat: Foods high in Vitamin E and C
avoid: High-glycemic processed foods (linked to acne flares)
Alcohol (can dehydrate skin and worsen psoriasis)
Common food allergens (peanuts, shellfish, if sensitive)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Engage in moderate exercise; wear loose, breathable cotton clothing to prevent sweat retention.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Get 7-8 hours of sleep; skin cells regenerate and repair damage primarily at night.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in stress relief (meditation, walking) as stress triggers flares in eczema, psoriasis, and acne.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink 2 to 2.5 liters of water daily to maintain epidermal hydration.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Acute rashes can clear in a week; chronic conditions like eczema have cycling flares and remissions requiring long-term management.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Avoid fragrance-containing lotions and laundry detergents. Apply thick ointments during winter.

faqs.question: What is the skin barrier?
faqs.answer: The skin barrier is the outermost layer of the skin, consisting of lipids and cells that protect the body from moisture loss and external irritants.

faqs.question: Does stress affect skin conditions?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress releases hormones like cortisol that trigger inflammatory cascades, worsening eczema, psoriasis, and acne.

faqs.question: How can I tell if a mole is cancerous?
faqs.answer: Use the ABCDE rule: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color changes, Diameter >6mm, and Evolving/changing over time. Consult a doctor if present.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dry skin and eczema?
faqs.answer: Dry skin is lack of moisture, while eczema is an inflammatory skin disease characterized by intense itching, red patches, and barrier dysfunction.

faqs.question: Why is scratching bad for a rash?
faqs.answer: Scratching damages the skin barrier further, increases local inflammation, and introduces bacteria from fingernails, risking secondary infections.

awareness_tips: Use broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen daily, even on cloudy days.
Moisturize your skin within three minutes of exiting the shower to lock in moisture.
Examine your moles monthly and report any changes using the ABCDE guidelines.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

Wash hands regularly and maintain hygiene

name: Melanoma
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of skin diseases.
overview: Melanoma is a condition affecting the skin, the body's largest organ. It can involve inflammation, infection, allergic reactions, or abnormal cellular growths. It affects the body by compromising the skin barrier, causing itching, pain, rashes, scaling, or cosmetic distress, and making the body more vulnerable to secondary infections.

causes_detail.primary: Epidermal barrier dysfunction, autoimmune cellular rapid growth, or pathogen infestation causing Melanoma.
causes_detail.secondary: Allergic contact sensitization, fungal colonization, or genetic skin cell overproduction.
causes_detail.environmental: Exposure to harsh chemicals, allergens, UV radiation, dry climates, or friction.
causes_detail.genetic: Hereditary factors influence conditions like Eczema, Psoriasis, and skin cancer susceptibility.

severe: Signs of skin infection (pus, warmth, red streaks)
Fever or chills accompanying skin peeling

risk_factors_detail.age: Acne is common in adolescents; eczema is frequent in children; skin cancers and shingles are more common in older adults.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: Frequent outdoor sun exposure without sunscreen, smoking, use of harsh soaps, and high stress levels.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: Family history of eczema, asthma, allergies, psoriasis, or melanoma.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: History of autoimmune disorders, chronic allergies, weakened immune system, or poor circulation.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Working with industrial chemicals, living in extremely dry or hot/humid climates, or exposure to allergens like pollen or pet dander.

complications.untreated: Secondary bacterial infections (cellulitis, impetigo), severe scarring, chronic pain, or systemic infection.
complications.long_term: Chronic skin thickening (lichenification), permanent pigment changes, sleep loss due to itching, or metastatic cancer.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen daily; avoid tanning beds; wear protective clothing outdoors.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Bathe daily with gentle, soap-free cleansers; moisturize immediately after bathing; do not share towels.
prevention_detail.vaccination: The Shingles (Zoster) vaccine prevents shingles outbreaks and chronic post-herpetic neuralgia.
prevention_detail.dietary: Stay hydrated and consume foods rich in healthy fats (Omega-3s) to support the lipid skin barrier.
prevention_detail.exercise: Shower promptly after sweating to prevent fungal or bacterial overgrowth on the skin.

precautions_detail.daily: Moisturize skin twice daily; perform self-skin checks; avoid scratching active lesions.
precautions_detail.travel: Pack hypoallergenic toiletries; bring adequate sun protection; carry hydrocortisone or prescribed topical creams.
precautions_detail.community: Promote sun safety in schools and participate in free skin cancer screening camps.

treatment_detail.first_line: Topical moisturizers, barrier creams, and mild topical hydrocortisone.
treatment_detail.home_care: Take lukewarm baths, use cool compresses for itching, and keep fingernails short to prevent scratching.
treatment_detail.medical: Prescription topical steroids, oral antihistamines, phototherapy, or systemic biologic therapies for severe autoimmune conditions.

when_to_consult: Consult a dermatologist for a rash that does not respond to OTC creams in 2 weeks, skin lesions that bleed, or changing moles.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care if a rash covers your entire body rapidly, is accompanied by high fever, begins blistering, or causes difficulty breathing.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Visual inspection of lesions, checking patterns and locations, and evaluating nail and scalp health.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Skin scraping for fungal culture, patch testing for contact allergies, or bacterial wound swab.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Dermoscopy (magnified skin inspection); rarely, ultrasound to check for deep abscesses.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Skin biopsy (taking a small tissue sample for lab analysis) and annual full-body mole mapping.

eat: Foods high in Vitamin E and C
avoid: High-glycemic processed foods (linked to acne flares)
Alcohol (can dehydrate skin and worsen psoriasis)
Common food allergens (peanuts, shellfish, if sensitive)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Engage in moderate exercise; wear loose, breathable cotton clothing to prevent sweat retention.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Get 7-8 hours of sleep; skin cells regenerate and repair damage primarily at night.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in stress relief (meditation, walking) as stress triggers flares in eczema, psoriasis, and acne.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink 2 to 2.5 liters of water daily to maintain epidermal hydration.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Acute rashes can clear in a week; chronic conditions like eczema have cycling flares and remissions requiring long-term management.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Avoid fragrance-containing lotions and laundry detergents. Apply thick ointments during winter.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen daily; avoid tanning beds; wear protective clothing outdoors.

prevention_detail.vaccination: The Shingles (Zoster) vaccine prevents shingles outbreaks and chronic post-herpetic neuralgia.
prevention_detail.dietary: Stay hydrated and consume foods rich in healthy fats (Omega-3s) to support the lipid skin barrier.
prevention_detail.exercise: Shower promptly after sweating to prevent fungal or bacterial overgrowth on the skin.

precautions_detail.daily: Moisturize skin twice daily; perform self-skin checks; avoid scratching active lesions.
precautions_detail.travel: Pack hypoallergenic toiletries; bring adequate sun protection; carry hydrocortisone or prescribed topical creams.
precautions_detail.community: Promote sun safety in schools and participate in free skin cancer screening camps.

treatment_detail.first_line: Topical moisturizers, barrier creams, and mild topical hydrocortisone.
treatment_detail.home_care: Take lukewarm baths, use cool compresses for itching, and keep fingernails short to prevent scratching.
treatment_detail.medical: Prescription topical steroids, oral antihistamines, phototherapy, or systemic biologic therapies for severe autoimmune conditions.

when_to_consult: Consult a dermatologist for a rash that does not respond to OTC creams in 2 weeks, skin lesions that bleed, or changing moles.

eat: Foods high in Vitamin E and C
avoid: High-glycemic processed foods (linked to acne flares)

faqs.question: What is the skin barrier?
faqs.answer: The skin barrier is the outermost layer of the skin, consisting of lipids and cells that protect the body from moisture loss and external irritants.

faqs.question: Does stress affect skin conditions?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress releases hormones like cortisol that trigger inflammatory cascades, worsening eczema, psoriasis, and acne.

faqs.question: How can I tell if a mole is cancerous?
faqs.answer: Use the ABCDE rule: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color changes, Diameter >6mm, and Evolving/changing over time. Consult a doctor if present.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dry skin and eczema?
faqs.answer: Dry skin is lack of moisture, while eczema is an inflammatory skin disease characterized by intense itching, red patches, and barrier dysfunction.

faqs.question: Why is scratching bad for a rash?
faqs.answer: Scratching damages the skin barrier further, increases local inflammation, and introduces bacteria from fingernails, risking secondary infections.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dry skin and eczema?
faqs.answer: Dry skin is lack of moisture, while eczema is an inflammatory skin disease characterized by intense itching, red patches, and barrier dysfunction.

faqs.question: Why is scratching bad for a rash?
faqs.answer: Scratching damages the skin barrier further, increases local inflammation, and introduces bacteria from fingernails, risking secondary infections.

awareness_tips: Use broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen daily, even on cloudy days.
Moisturize your skin within three minutes of exiting the shower to lock in moisture.
Examine your moles monthly and report any changes using the ABCDE guidelines.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.
description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of skin diseases.
causes: Genetic predisposition factors associated with Hives
Environmental triggers and exposure related to Hives
Immune system irregularities
precautions: Stay hydrated and get adequate rest
Consult a physician if symptoms worsen
prevention: Maintain a balanced, healthy lifestyle
Routine screening and checkups with healthcare providers
Wash hands regularly and maintain hygiene
basic_treatment: Symptomatic support (adequate hydration, rest)
Over-the-counter pain relief as advised by doctors
Therapy or specialist referral depending on severity

name: Gout
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of bone & joint diseases.
overview: Gout is a disorder of the musculoskeletal system, which includes bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and cartilage. It involves degeneration of joint cushions, systemic inflammation, or loss of bone mineral density. It affects the body by causing joint pain, stiffness, skeletal fragility, and restricted range of motion, reducing overall mobility.

causes_detail.primary: Breakdown of joint cartilage, autoimmune attack on joint linings, or mineral depletion in bones as in Gout.
causes_detail.secondary: Repetitive mechanical load, wear-and-tear, or metabolic crystal deposition (gout).
causes_detail.environmental: Sedentary lifestyle, repetitive occupational joint stress, or lack of dietary calcium/Vitamin D.
causes_detail.genetic: Genetic factors influence bone structure, inflammatory arthritis risk, and peak bone density levels.

early: Joint stiffness in the morning or after resting
Mild joint ache after prolonged use
severe: Joint deformity or inability to bear weight
Severe, localized joint swelling, warmth, and redness
Sudden bone fracture from minor fall
Fever and chills accompanying joint pain

risk_factors_detail.age: Osteoporosis and osteoarthritis risks rise sharply in older adults, while rheumatoid arthritis often manifests between ages 30-50.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: Sedentary lifestyle, smoking (accelerates bone loss), poor diet lacking calcium, and high-impact repetitive strain.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: Family history of osteoporosis, early-onset arthritis, or autoimmune joint disorders.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Obesity (places heavy load on joints), diabetes, thyroid disease, or chronic use of steroid medications.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Occupations requiring repetitive kneeling, heavy lifting, or exposure to damp, cold climates.

complications.untreated: Severe chronic pain, joint deformities, loss of mobility, or high risk of hip/spine fractures.
complications.long_term: Permanent loss of joint function, dependence on walking aids, chronic inflammatory state, or severe curvature of the spine.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Maintain a healthy weight; avoid smoking; wear supportive footwear; practice correct ergonomics.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Keep good posture; avoid joint-straining postures; use assistive items when lifting heavy weights.
prevention_detail.vaccination: None available, but staying active keeps overall immune health stable in inflammatory arthritis.
prevention_detail.dietary: Ensure adequate calcium and Vitamin D intake; eat anti-inflammatory foods.
prevention_detail.exercise: Perform low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling) and strength training to build supportive muscles.

precautions_detail.daily: Perform joint-friendly stretches daily; avoid high-impact activities if joints are inflamed; apply heat for stiffness and ice for swelling.
precautions_detail.travel: Use luggage with wheels; take stretching breaks during long travel; carry folding walking aids if needed.
precautions_detail.community: Advocate for step-free access, ramps, and fall-prevention exercise classes for older adults.

treatment_detail.first_line: Pain relievers (Acetaminophen, NSAIDs), physical therapy, and joint protection measures.
treatment_detail.home_care: Use hot/cold packs, apply topical pain-relief gels, perform gentle range-of-motion exercises, and rest when joints flare.
treatment_detail.medical: Corticosteroid injections, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), joint-viscosupplementation, or joint replacement surgery.

when_to_consult: Consult a rheumatologist or orthopedist for joint pain lasting over 6 weeks, unexplained morning stiffness, or swollen, warm joints.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care for an inability to move a joint, suspected bone fracture, or sudden severe joint swelling accompanied by high fever.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Evaluating range of motion, checking for joint tenderness and swelling, testing muscle strength, and checking spinal alignment.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Rheumatoid Factor (RF), Anti-CCP antibodies, Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR/CRP), or uric acid levels.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Joint X-rays to check joint space narrowing/bone spurs; MRI for soft-tissue ligament damage.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Dual-Energy X-ray Absorptiometry (DEXA) scan to measure bone mineral density.

eat: Fortified cereals and plant milks (Vitamin D)
avoid: Excessive salt (can cause calcium loss from bones)
Excessive alcohol and caffeine (impair calcium absorption)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Combine resistance training (strengthens bone) with low-impact cardio. Perform balance training.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Aim for 8 hours; rest is essential for joint tissues to repair and recover from daily mechanical strain.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in meditation as chronic stress can worsen pain perception in joint conditions.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink 2 liters of water; joint cartilage is composed mostly of water and needs hydration to remain lubricated.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Degenerative conditions are managed long-term; post-joint replacement rehab takes 3-6 months for optimal recovery.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Regularly check home environments for fall hazards. Avoid high-impact loading on arthritic joints.

faqs.question: What is the difference between osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis?
faqs.answer: Osteoarthritis is a wear-and-tear disease where joint cartilage breaks down, while rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks joint linings.

faqs.question: Why is Vitamin D important for bones?
faqs.answer: Vitamin D is essential for the body to absorb calcium from food. Without adequate Vitamin D, the body cannot build strong bones, regardless of calcium intake.

faqs.question: How does excess weight affect joint health?
faqs.answer: Every pound of excess weight puts four additional pounds of pressure on knee joints, accelerating cartilage breakdown.

faqs.question: What is a DEXA scan?
faqs.answer: A DEXA scan is a low-radiation X-ray that measures bone mineral density. It is the gold standard for diagnosing osteopenia and osteoporosis.

faqs.question: Can joint cracking cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: No, popping knuckles release gas bubbles from joint fluid and has not been shown to cause arthritis, though habitual cracking may reduce grip strength.

awareness_tips: Incorporate weight-bearing exercises to maintain bone density.
Ensure adequate daily calcium (1000-1200 mg) and Vitamin D (600-800 IU).
Set up your workspace ergonomically to prevent neck and back joint strain.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

risk_factors_detail.family_history: Family history of osteoporosis, early-onset arthritis, or autoimmune joint disorders.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Obesity (places heavy load on joints), diabetes, thyroid disease, or chronic use of steroid medications.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Occupations requiring repetitive kneeling, heavy lifting, or exposure to damp, cold climates.

complications.untreated: Severe chronic pain, joint deformities, loss of mobility, or high risk of hip/spine fractures.
complications.long_term: Permanent loss of joint function, dependence on walking aids, chronic inflammatory state, or severe curvature of the spine.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Maintain a healthy weight; avoid smoking; wear supportive footwear; practice correct ergonomics.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Keep good posture; avoid joint-straining postures; use assistive items when lifting heavy weights.
prevention_detail.vaccination: None available, but staying active keeps overall immune health stable in inflammatory arthritis.
prevention_detail.dietary: Ensure adequate calcium and Vitamin D intake; eat anti-inflammatory foods.
prevention_detail.exercise: Perform low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling) and strength training to build supportive muscles.

precautions_detail.daily: Perform joint-friendly stretches daily; avoid high-impact activities if joints are inflamed; apply heat for stiffness and ice for swelling.
precautions_detail.travel: Use luggage with wheels; take stretching breaks during long travel; carry folding walking aids if needed.
precautions_detail.community: Advocate for step-free access, ramps, and fall-prevention exercise classes for older adults.

treatment_detail.first_line: Pain relievers (Acetaminophen, NSAIDs), physical therapy, and joint protection measures.
treatment_detail.home_care: Use hot/cold packs, apply topical pain-relief gels, perform gentle range-of-motion exercises, and rest when joints flare.
treatment_detail.medical: Corticosteroid injections, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), joint-viscosupplementation, or joint replacement surgery.

when_to_consult: Consult a rheumatologist or orthopedist for joint pain lasting over 6 weeks, unexplained morning stiffness, or swollen, warm joints.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care for an inability to move a joint, suspected bone fracture, or sudden severe joint swelling accompanied by high fever.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Evaluating range of motion, checking for joint tenderness and swelling, testing muscle strength, and checking spinal alignment.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Rheumatoid Factor (RF), Anti-CCP antibodies, Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR/CRP), or uric acid levels.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Joint X-rays to check joint space narrowing/bone spurs; MRI for soft-tissue ligament damage.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Dual-Energy X-ray Absorptiometry (DEXA) scan to measure bone mineral density.

eat: Fortified cereals and plant milks (Vitamin D)
avoid: Excessive salt (can cause calcium loss from bones)
Excessive alcohol and caffeine (impair calcium absorption)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Combine resistance training (strengthens bone) with low-impact cardio. Perform balance training.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Aim for 8 hours; rest is essential for joint tissues to repair and recover from daily mechanical strain.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in meditation as chronic stress can worsen pain perception in joint conditions.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink 2 liters of water; joint cartilage is composed mostly of water and needs hydration to remain lubricated.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Degenerative conditions are managed long-term; post-joint replacement rehab takes 3-6 months for optimal recovery.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Regularly check home environments for fall hazards. Avoid high-impact loading on arthritic joints.

faqs.question: What is the difference between osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis?
faqs.answer: Osteoarthritis is a wear-and-tear disease where joint cartilage breaks down, while rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks joint linings.

faqs.question: Why is Vitamin D important for bones?
faqs.answer: Vitamin D is essential for the body to absorb calcium from food. Without adequate Vitamin D, the body cannot build strong bones, regardless of calcium intake.

faqs.question: How does excess weight affect joint health?
faqs.answer: Every pound of excess weight puts four additional pounds of pressure on knee joints, accelerating cartilage breakdown.

faqs.question: What is a DEXA scan?
faqs.answer: A DEXA scan is a low-radiation X-ray that measures bone mineral density. It is the gold standard for diagnosing osteopenia and osteoporosis.

faqs.question: Can joint cracking cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: No, popping knuckles release gas bubbles from joint fluid and has not been shown to cause arthritis, though habitual cracking may reduce grip strength.

awareness_tips: Incorporate weight-bearing exercises to maintain bone density.
Ensure adequate daily calcium (1000-1200 mg) and Vitamin D (600-800 IU).
Set up your workspace ergonomically to prevent neck and back joint strain.

name: Bursitis
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of bone & joint diseases.
overview: Bursitis is a disorder of the musculoskeletal system, which includes bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and cartilage. eat: Fortified cereals and plant milks (Vitamin D)
avoid: Excessive salt (can cause calcium loss from bones)
Excessive alcohol and caffeine (impair calcium absorption)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Combine resistance training (strengthens bone) with low-impact cardio. Perform balance training.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Aim for 8 hours; rest is essential for joint tissues to repair and recover from daily mechanical strain.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in meditation as chronic stress can worsen pain perception in joint conditions.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink 2 liters of water; joint cartilage is composed mostly of water and needs hydration to remain lubricated.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Degenerative conditions are managed long-term; post-joint replacement rehab takes 3-6 months for optimal recovery.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Regularly check home environments for fall hazards. Avoid high-impact loading on arthritic joints.

faqs.question: What is the difference between osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis?
faqs.answer: Osteoarthritis is a wear-and-tear disease where joint cartilage breaks down, while rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks joint linings.

faqs.question: Why is Vitamin D important for bones?
faqs.answer: Vitamin D is essential for the body to absorb calcium from food. Without adequate Vitamin D, the body cannot build strong bones, regardless of calcium intake.

faqs.question: How does excess weight affect joint health?
faqs.answer: Every pound of excess weight puts four additional pounds of pressure on knee joints, accelerating cartilage breakdown.

faqs.question: What is a DEXA scan?
faqs.answer: A DEXA scan is a low-radiation X-ray that measures bone mineral density. It is the gold standard for diagnosing osteopenia and osteoporosis.

faqs.question: Can joint cracking cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: No, popping knuckles release gas bubbles from joint fluid and has not been shown to cause arthritis, though habitual cracking may reduce grip strength.

awareness_tips: Incorporate weight-bearing exercises to maintain bone density.
Ensure adequate daily calcium (1000-1200 mg) and Vitamin D (600-800 IU).
Set up your workspace ergonomically to prevent neck and back joint strain.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

name: Schizophrenia
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of mental health disorders.
overview: Schizophrenia is a mental health condition that affects emotion, thinking, behavior, or relationships. It involves disruptions in neurotransmitter regulation, brain circuitry, or psychological coping mechanisms. It affects the body by causing cognitive deficits, sleep disturbances, fatigue, altered appetite, and significant distress or impairment in daily functioning.

causes_detail.primary: Disruptions in brain chemistry (serotonin, dopamine), genetic predisposition, or traumatic life events in Schizophrenia.
causes_detail.secondary: Chronic stress, hormonal shifts, or adverse childhood experiences.
causes_detail.environmental: Social isolation, high-stress workplaces, history of abuse, or substance abuse.
causes_detail.genetic: High family heritability; family history of mood or thought disorders increases individual vulnerability.

early: Changes in sleeping habits (insomnia or oversleeping)
Withdrawal from social activities and friends
Unusual irritability or sudden mood shifts
Decreased performance at work or school
severe: Severe panic attacks causing functional paralysis

risk_factors_detail.age: Can emerge at any age; many psychiatric disorders manifest in late adolescence or early adulthood.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: Chronic sleep deprivation, sedentary habits, poor diet, isolation, and use of alcohol or recreational drugs.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: First-degree relative with depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, or anxiety disorders.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Chronic pain, terminal illness, neurological conditions, or hormone imbalances.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Experiencing trauma, domestic violence, systemic discrimination, poverty, or severe loss.

complications.untreated: Severe functional impairment, social isolation, substance addiction, worsening physical health, or self-harm/suicide.
complications.long_term: Chronic social difficulties, unemployment, cardiovascular strain from chronic stress, and shortened life expectancy.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Maintain stable sleep schedules, cultivate supportive friendships, and limit alcohol and stimulants.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Practice 'mental hygiene': set boundaries, take screen breaks, and engage in hobbies.
prevention_detail.vaccination: None available, but treating infections promptly protects neurological systems.
prevention_detail.dietary: Eat a nutrient-rich diet with complex carbohydrates and healthy fats (Omega-3s) to support brain health.
prevention_detail.exercise: Engage in regular aerobic exercise; physical activity releases endorphins and reduces stress hormones.

precautions_detail.daily: Follow medication schedules carefully; practice daily mindfulness or journal mood changes; maintain contact with support systems.
precautions_detail.travel: Bring adequate medications and copies of prescriptions; travel with a companion if prone to panic; plan relaxation periods.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in mental health stigma reduction and support community counseling services.

treatment_detail.first_line: Psychotherapy (e.g., Cognitive Behavioral Therapy - CBT) and lifestyle adjustments.
treatment_detail.home_care: Keep a structured routine, practice meditation, use sleep hygiene techniques, and avoid isolating yourself.
treatment_detail.medical: Psychiatric consultation, prescription antidepressants, mood stabilizers, or anti-anxiety medications.

when_to_consult: Consult a therapist or psychiatrist if you experience persistent sadness, worry, or mood shifts lasting over 2 weeks that affect daily life.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency psychiatric care or call a crisis hotline immediately if you experience active thoughts of suicide, severe delusions, or extreme self-harm impulses.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Evaluation to rule out physical causes of symptoms (such as thyroid disorders or vitamin deficiencies).
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Complete thyroid panel, Vitamin D and B12 levels, and toxic screening tests.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Brain scans (CT or MRI) are only used if a structural lesion or stroke is suspected of causing behavioral changes.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Standardized clinical questionnaires (e.g., PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety) and diagnostic interviews.

eat: Eggs, lean poultry, and fish (tryptophan sources)
Fermented foods (kefir, yogurt) for gut-brain support
avoid: Refined sugars and sweets (cause energy crashes)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Perform 30 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walk) daily to help regulate mood neurotransmitters.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Set a regular bedtime; aim for 7.5 to 8 hours of sleep, as sleep is key for emotional processing.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in progressive muscle relaxation, journaling, and deep breathing daily.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink water; dehydration can affect concentration, fatigue levels, and mood regulation.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Recovery is an active process; symptoms can improve significantly within 4-6 weeks of starting therapy or medication.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Build a relapse prevention plan with a therapist. Keep a list of emergency contacts handy.

faqs.question: What is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)?
faqs.answer: CBT is a common form of talk therapy that helps you identify and change negative or unhelpful thought and behavior patterns.

faqs.question: Do mental health medications change my personality?
faqs.answer: No, psychiatric medications are designed to balance brain chemistry and reduce distressing symptoms, not alter your core identity.

faqs.question: Is depression just feeling sad?
faqs.answer: No, sadness is temporary. Depression is a medical illness characterized by persistent low mood, loss of interest, fatigue, and other cognitive symptoms lasting weeks.

faqs.question: How does exercise help anxiety?
faqs.answer: Exercise releases endorphins, burns off stress hormones (adrenaline/cortisol), and redirects focus away from anxious thoughts.

faqs.question: How can I support a loved one with a mental health condition?
faqs.answer: Listen without judgment, offer practical support, encourage professional help, and avoid offering simplistic advice.

awareness_tips: Talk openly about mental health to reduce stigma and encourage others to seek help.
Establish healthy boundaries at work and home to protect your mental well-being.
Check in on your friends and family; a simple conversation can make a huge difference.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

risk_factors_detail.environmental: Experiencing trauma, domestic violence, systemic discrimination, poverty, or severe loss.

complications.untreated: Severe functional impairment, social isolation, substance addiction, worsening physical health, or self-harm/suicide.
complications.long_term: Chronic social difficulties, unemployment, cardiovascular strain from chronic stress, and shortened life expectancy.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Maintain stable sleep schedules, cultivate supportive friendships, and limit alcohol and stimulants.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Practice 'mental hygiene': set boundaries, take screen breaks, and engage in hobbies.
prevention_detail.vaccination: None available, but treating infections promptly protects neurological systems.
prevention_detail.dietary: Eat a nutrient-rich diet with complex carbohydrates and healthy fats (Omega-3s) to support brain health.
prevention_detail.exercise: Engage in regular aerobic exercise; physical activity releases endorphins and reduces stress hormones.

precautions_detail.daily: Follow medication schedules carefully; practice daily mindfulness or journal mood changes; maintain contact with support systems.
precautions_detail.travel: Bring adequate medications and copies of prescriptions; travel with a companion if prone to panic; plan relaxation periods.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in mental health stigma reduction and support community counseling services.

treatment_detail.first_line: Psychotherapy (e.g., Cognitive Behavioral Therapy - CBT) and lifestyle adjustments.
treatment_detail.home_care: Keep a structured routine, practice meditation, use sleep hygiene techniques, and avoid isolating yourself.
treatment_detail.medical: Psychiatric consultation, prescription antidepressants, mood stabilizers, or anti-anxiety medications.

when_to_consult: Consult a therapist or psychiatrist if you experience persistent sadness, worry, or mood shifts lasting over 2 weeks that affect daily life.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency psychiatric care or call a crisis hotline immediately if you experience active thoughts of suicide, severe delusions, or extreme self-harm impulses.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Evaluation to rule out physical causes of symptoms (such as thyroid disorders or vitamin deficiencies).
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Complete thyroid panel, Vitamin D and B12 levels, and toxic screening tests.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Brain scans (CT or MRI) are only used if a structural lesion or stroke is suspected of causing behavioral changes.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Standardized clinical questionnaires (e.g., PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety) and diagnostic interviews.

eat: Eggs, lean poultry, and fish (tryptophan sources)
Fermented foods (kefir, yogurt) for gut-brain support
avoid: Refined sugars and sweets (cause energy crashes)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Perform 30 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walk) daily to help regulate mood neurotransmitters.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Set a regular bedtime; aim for 7.5 to 8 hours of sleep, as sleep is key for emotional processing.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in progressive muscle relaxation, journaling, and deep breathing daily.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink water; dehydration can affect concentration, fatigue levels, and mood regulation.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Recovery is an active process; symptoms can improve significantly within 4-6 weeks of starting therapy or medication.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Build a relapse prevention plan with a therapist. Keep a list of emergency contacts handy.

faqs.question: What is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)?
faqs.answer: CBT is a common form of talk therapy that helps you identify and change negative or unhelpful thought and behavior patterns.

faqs.question: Do mental health medications change my personality?
faqs.answer: No, psychiatric medications are designed to balance brain chemistry and reduce distressing symptoms, not alter your core identity.

faqs.question: Is depression just feeling sad?
faqs.answer: No, sadness is temporary. Depression is a medical illness characterized by persistent low mood, loss of interest, fatigue, and other cognitive symptoms lasting weeks.

faqs.question: How does exercise help anxiety?
faqs.answer: Exercise releases endorphins, burns off stress hormones (adrenaline/cortisol), and redirects focus away from anxious thoughts.

faqs.question: How can I support a loved one with a mental health condition?
faqs.answer: Listen without judgment, offer practical support, encourage professional help, and avoid offering simplistic advice.

awareness_tips: Talk openly about mental health to reduce stigma and encourage others to seek help.
Establish healthy boundaries at work and home to protect your mental well-being.
Check in on your friends and family; a simple conversation can make a huge difference.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

name: Social Anxiety Disorder
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of mental health disorders.
overview: Social Anxiety Disorder is a mental health condition that affects emotion, thinking, behavior, or relationships. name: Insomnia Disorder
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of mental health disorders.
overview: Insomnia Disorder is a mental health condition that affects emotion, thinking, behavior, or relationships. It involves disruptions in neurotransmitter regulation, brain circuitry, or psychological coping mechanisms. It affects the body by causing cognitive deficits, sleep disturbances, fatigue, altered appetite, and significant distress or impairment in daily functioning.

causes_detail.primary: Disruptions in brain chemistry (serotonin, dopamine), genetic predisposition, or traumatic life events in Insomnia Disorder.
causes_detail.secondary: Chronic stress, hormonal shifts, or adverse childhood experiences.
causes_detail.environmental: Social isolation, high-stress workplaces, history of abuse, or substance abuse.
causes_detail.genetic: High family heritability; family history of mood or thought disorders increases individual vulnerability.

early: Changes in sleeping habits (insomnia or oversleeping)
Withdrawal from social activities and friends
Unusual irritability or sudden mood shifts
Decreased performance at work or school
common: Pain in areas surrounding Insomnia Disorder
severe: Severe panic attacks causing functional paralysis

risk_factors_detail.age: Can emerge at any age; many psychiatric disorders manifest in late adolescence or early adulthood.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: Chronic sleep deprivation, sedentary habits, poor diet, isolation, and use of alcohol or recreational drugs.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: First-degree relative with depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, or anxiety disorders.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Chronic pain, terminal illness, neurological conditions, or hormone imbalances.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Experiencing trauma, domestic violence, systemic discrimination, poverty, or severe loss.

complications.untreated: Severe functional impairment, social isolation, substance addiction, worsening physical health, or self-harm/suicide.
complications.long_term: Chronic social difficulties, unemployment, cardiovascular strain from chronic stress, and shortened life expectancy.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Maintain stable sleep schedules, cultivate supportive friendships, and limit alcohol and stimulants.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Practice 'mental hygiene': set boundaries, take screen breaks, and engage in hobbies.
prevention_detail.vaccination: None available, but treating infections promptly protects neurological systems.
prevention_detail.dietary: Eat a nutrient-rich diet with complex carbohydrates and healthy fats (Omega-3s) to support brain health.
prevention_detail.exercise: Engage in regular aerobic exercise; physical activity releases endorphins and reduces stress hormones.

precautions_detail.daily: Follow medication schedules carefully; practice daily mindfulness or journal mood changes; maintain contact with support systems.
precautions_detail.travel: Bring adequate medications and copies of prescriptions; travel with a companion if prone to panic; plan relaxation periods.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in mental health stigma reduction and support community counseling services.

treatment_detail.first_line: Psychotherapy (e.g., Cognitive Behavioral Therapy - CBT) and lifestyle adjustments.
treatment_detail.home_care: Keep a structured routine, practice meditation, use sleep hygiene techniques, and avoid isolating yourself.
treatment_detail.medical: Psychiatric consultation, prescription antidepressants, mood stabilizers, or anti-anxiety medications.

when_to_consult: Consult a therapist or psychiatrist if you experience persistent sadness, worry, or mood shifts lasting over 2 weeks that affect daily life.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency psychiatric care or call a crisis hotline immediately if you experience active thoughts of suicide, severe delusions, or extreme self-harm impulses.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Evaluation to rule out physical causes of symptoms (such as thyroid disorders or vitamin deficiencies).
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Complete thyroid panel, Vitamin D and B12 levels, and toxic screening tests.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: Brain scans (CT or MRI) are only used if a structural lesion or stroke is suspected of causing behavioral changes.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Standardized clinical questionnaires (e.g., PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety) and diagnostic interviews.

eat: Eggs, lean poultry, and fish (tryptophan sources)
Fermented foods (kefir, yogurt) for gut-brain support
avoid: Refined sugars and sweets (cause energy crashes)

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Perform 30 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walk) daily to help regulate mood neurotransmitters.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Set a regular bedtime; aim for 7.5 to 8 hours of sleep, as sleep is key for emotional processing.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in progressive muscle relaxation, journaling, and deep breathing daily.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink water; dehydration can affect concentration, fatigue levels, and mood regulation.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Recovery is an active process; symptoms can improve significantly within 4-6 weeks of starting therapy or medication.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Build a relapse prevention plan with a therapist. Keep a list of emergency contacts handy.

faqs.question: What is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)?
faqs.answer: CBT is a common form of talk therapy that helps you identify and change negative or unhelpful thought and behavior patterns.

faqs.question: Do mental health medications change my personality?
faqs.answer: No, psychiatric medications are designed to balance brain chemistry and reduce distressing symptoms, not alter your core identity.

faqs.question: Is depression just feeling sad?
faqs.answer: No, sadness is temporary. Depression is a medical illness characterized by persistent low mood, loss of interest, fatigue, and other cognitive symptoms lasting weeks.

faqs.question: How does exercise help anxiety?
faqs.answer: Exercise releases endorphins, burns off stress hormones (adrenaline/cortisol), and redirects focus away from anxious thoughts.

faqs.question: How can I support a loved one with a mental health condition?
faqs.answer: Listen without judgment, offer practical support, encourage professional help, and avoid offering simplistic advice.

awareness_tips: Talk openly about mental health to reduce stigma and encourage others to seek help.
Establish healthy boundaries at work and home to protect your mental well-being.
Check in on your friends and family; a simple conversation can make a huge difference.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.

name: Prostate Cancer
short_description: A clinical condition characterized by abnormal functions or changes in bodily tissues, specifically presenting under the category of cancer types.
overview: Prostate Cancer is a disease characterized by the uncontrolled growth and spread of abnormal cells. It typically affects the body when cells acquire genetic mutations that override normal cell cycle controls, leading to tumor formation, tissue invasion, and metastasis via blood or lymph systems. It compromises vital organ functions, causing fatigue, cachexia, and localized dysfunction.

causes_detail.primary: DNA mutations in cells leading to unchecked proliferation, as seen in Prostate Cancer.
causes_detail.secondary: Inability of the immune system to recognize and destroy mutant cells.
causes_detail.environmental: Exposure to carcinogens like tobacco smoke, radiation, asbestos, chemical toxins, or chronic infections (HPV, H. pylori).
causes_detail.genetic: Inherited mutations (such as BRCA1/2, Lynch Syndrome) that impair DNA repair systems.

early: Unexplained weight loss (>10 lbs in a month)
Persistent unusual fatigue or low energy
common: Pain in areas surrounding Prostate Cancer
severe: Severe localized pain unresponsive to medications
Severe confusion or seizures due to organ involvement

risk_factors_detail.age: The risk increases significantly with age; the majority of cancers are diagnosed in individuals aged 50 and older.
risk_factors_detail.lifestyle: Tobacco smoking, heavy alcohol consumption, poor diet low in fiber/vegetables, obesity, and lack of exercise.
risk_factors_detail.family_history: Multiple close relatives diagnosed with the same or related cancers, especially at an early age.
risk_factors_detail.medical_conditions: Chronic inflammatory conditions (like ulcerative colitis), chronic viral infections (Hepatitis B/C), or weakened immunity.
risk_factors_detail.environmental: Prolonged occupational exposure to industrial chemicals, solar UV radiation without protection, or high radon levels.

complications.untreated: Metastasis (spread to other organs), organ failure, severe wasting syndrome (cachexia), or death.
complications.long_term: Chronic fatigue, immune suppression, lymphedema, pain, or potential recurrence.

prevention_detail.lifestyle: Do not smoke; avoid secondhand smoke; limit alcohol; protect skin from UV exposure.
prevention_detail.hygiene: Practice food safety; protect against sexual transmission of oncogenic viruses (HPV, Hepatitis).
prevention_detail.vaccination: Receive the HPV vaccine to prevent cervical and throat cancers, and the Hepatitis B vaccine to protect the liver.
prevention_detail.dietary: Eat a plant-based diet high in fiber, antioxidant-rich fruits, cruciferous vegetables, and low in processed meats.
prevention_detail.exercise: Maintain regular physical activity (at least 30 minutes a day) to regulate hormones and reduce obesity risks.

precautions_detail.daily: Perform regular self-exams; protect sensitive skin from sun; avoid exposure to industrial carcinogens.
precautions_detail.travel: Ensure travel plans accommodate treatment schedules; bring complete medical records, summaries, and medication lists.
precautions_detail.community: Participate in public health cancer screening campaigns and advocate for carcinogen regulations.

treatment_detail.first_line: Surgical removal of localized tumors, chemotherapy, or radiation therapy.
treatment_detail.home_care: Manage treatment side effects (nausea, fatigue), maintain nutrition with calorie-dense soft foods, and use infection precautions.
treatment_detail.medical: Targeted therapy, immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors), hormonal therapy, or bone marrow transplantation.

when_to_consult: Consult a healthcare provider for any lump that does not go away, unexplained weight loss, persistent cough, or changes in bowel habits.
emergency_warning_signs: Seek emergency care for high fever while on chemotherapy (indicating neutropenic fever), sudden shortness of breath, severe uncontrolled bleeding, or seizures.

diagnosis_methods.physical_exam: Palpating for lumps, checking lymph nodes, examining skin for irregular lesions, and inspecting organs.
diagnosis_methods.laboratory_tests: Complete Blood Count (CBC), tumor markers (e.g., PSA, CA-125), liver and kidney function tests.
diagnosis_methods.imaging_tests: CT scans, MRI, PET scans, mammography, and ultrasound.
diagnosis_methods.screening: Biopsy (the definitive method of collecting a tissue sample for histological analysis) and colonoscopy.

eat: Lean proteins (fish, chicken, beans) to maintain muscle mass
Garlic, ginger, and turmeric (natural anti-inflammatory features)
avoid: Excessive red meat and saturated fats

lifestyle_recommendations.exercise: Participate in moderate walking or gentle yoga as tolerated to combat cancer-related fatigue.
lifestyle_recommendations.sleep: Prioritize 8-9 hours of sleep; sleep is critical for immune cell recovery and tissue rebuilding.
lifestyle_recommendations.stress_management: Engage in support groups, meditation, and counseling to address emotional anxiety.
lifestyle_recommendations.hydration: Drink 2 to 3 liters of fluids daily to flush out chemotherapy byproducts and prevent dehydration.

recovery_management.expected_recovery: Recovery is highly individualized; depends on staging, cancer type, and treatment type, ranging from months to ongoing management.
recovery_management.long_term_tips: Attend all scheduled follow-up scans and checkups. Monitor for signs of recurrence or side effects.

faqs.question: What is 'metastasis'?
faqs.answer: Metastasis is the process where cancer cells break away from the primary tumor and travel through the blood or lymph system to form new tumors in other organs.

faqs.question: How does chemotherapy work?
faqs.answer: Chemotherapy uses powerful drugs to target and kill rapidly dividing cells, which includes cancer cells but also some healthy cells like hair follicles.

faqs.question: Is cancer hereditary?
faqs.answer: Only about 5-10% of cancers are hereditary (linked to inherited gene mutations). The majority occur due to accumulated mutations from aging and lifestyle factors.

faqs.question: What is immunotherapy?
faqs.answer: Immunotherapy is a class of treatment that helps your body's immune system recognize and attack cancer cells.

faqs.question: What is the difference between benign and malignant tumors?
faqs.answer: Benign tumors are non-cancerous and do not spread to nearby tissues. Malignant tumors are cancerous, can invade adjacent tissues, and spread throughout the body.

awareness_tips: Get screened regularly: undergo colonoscopies, mammograms, and pap smears.
Protect your skin: use SPF 30+ sunscreen and wear wide-brimmed hats.
Avoid all tobacco products; smoking causes about 85% of all lung cancers.
disclaimer: This information is provided for educational and disease awareness purposes only. It should not be considered medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional.
